Based on the haniwa and other artifacts, the mound's construction is estimated to date to the mid-6th century, making it significant for studying the late Kofun period in eastern Japan. == Site History == * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': Excavation due to residential development. Confirmed as a large-scale kofun (Iwaki City Board of Education). * '''2017 (Heisei 29)''': Survey conducted. Confirmed as the largest late Kofun in Tōhoku (Fukushima University Archaeological Research Laboratory).["Iwaki's Tsukamae Kofun: Largest Late Kofun in Tōhoku Revealed by Survey" (Mainichi Shimbun, May 11, 2017)]
["Tsukamae Kofun: Measuring 120m, Largest Late Tōhoku Kofun?" (Kahoku Shimpō, May 11, 2017)]. == Kofun Details == Measurements from the 2017 survey are as follows: * Mound length: 95–120 meters * Rear round portion: Diameter 53 meters * Front rectangular portion: ** Length: 54–70 meters ** Width: 45–65 meters The mound was constructed by alternating layers of earth to form embankments, then filling the inner area, a technique characteristic of western Japan. Additionally, soil bags and clod stacking were used, a method typically reserved for high-status tombs in the late Kofun period. == Notes == {{Notelist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} * {{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳}} (Yotsukura, Iwaki) - Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. 112-meter keyhole-shaped tumulus. * {{ill|Ishikami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造}} [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:Fukushima Prefecture history]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[Category:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] {{Kofun navbar}} [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[Category:S11]] [[Category:Q--q]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:s11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:s11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[ja:塚前古墳]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:sukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[en:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

revision.text:
'''Tsukamae Kofun''' (塚前古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Onahama Rinjo|ja|小名浜林城}}, {{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|ja|いわき市}}, [[Fukushima Prefecture]]. Its shape is that of a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}. It is not designated as a historic site. Among kofun in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|ja|東北地方}}, it ranks 3rd to 8th in sizeMajor kofun in the Tōhoku region include: # {{ill|Raijin-yama Kofun|ja|雷神山古墳}} (Natori, Miyagi) - 168 meters in length. # {{ill|Kamegamori-Chinjumori Kofun|ja|亀ヶ森・鎮守森古墳}} (Aizubange, Fukushima) - 129 meters in length. # {{ill|Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|会津大塚山古墳}} (Aizuwakamatsu, Fukushima) - 114 meters in length. # {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳}} (Iwaki, Fukushima) - 112 meters in length. # {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (Sendai, Miyagi) - 110 meters in length. # {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳}} (Ōsaki, Miyagi) - 100 meters in length. # {{ill|Inarimori Kofun|ja|稲荷森古墳}} (Nanyō, Yamagata) - 96 meters in length. Recent surveys suggest that other kofun, including Tsukamae Kofun (95–120 meters in length), may also be classified as large-scale tumuli: * {{ill|Minamimori Kofun|ja|南森古墳}} (Nanyō, Yamagata) - Estimated to be 150–168 meters in length. and is the largest kofun from the late {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} in the Tōhoku region, dated to the mid-6th century. == Overview == Tsukamae Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus located in the eastern part of Fukushima Prefecture, in the Hamadōri area, at the head of Onahama Bay on the alluvial plain of the right bank of the Yada River (a tributary of the Fujiwara River). The mound has been significantly leveled and eroded over time.{{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} Excavations conducted by the Iwaki City Board of Education in fiscal 2016 (Heisei 28) and a survey by Fukushima University revealed its scale and construction method.{{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} The tumulus, now irregularly shaped, is reconstructed as originally being keyhole-shaped, with the front portion oriented southwest. Its mound measures 95–120 meters in length, ranking 3rd to 8th in size among Tōhoku kofun and is the largest from the late Kofun period in the region. Remains of haniwa, including cylindrical haniwa (some morning glory-shaped) and figurative haniwa (lid and shield-shaped), have been found on the mound surface. A surrounding moat (likely horseshoe-shaped) has also been identified. While the burial facilities are unknown, past records suggest the presence of ornaments such as gyōba (杏葉). Based on the haniwa and other artifacts, the mound's construction is estimated to date to the mid-6th century, making it significant for studying the late Kofun period in eastern Japan. == Site History == * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': Excavation due to residential development. Confirmed as a large-scale kofun (Iwaki City Board of Education). * '''2017 (Heisei 29)''': Survey conducted. Confirmed as the largest late Kofun in Tōhoku (Fukushima University Archaeological Research Laboratory).["Iwaki's Tsukamae Kofun: Largest Late Kofun in Tōhoku Revealed by Survey" (Mainichi Shimbun, May 11, 2017)]
["Tsukamae Kofun: Measuring 120m, Largest Late Tōhoku Kofun?" (Kahoku Shimpō, May 11, 2017)]. == Kofun Details == Measurements from the 2017 survey are as follows: * Mound length: 95–120 meters * Rear round portion: Diameter 53 meters * Front rectangular portion: ** Length: 54–70 meters ** Width: 45–65 meters The mound was constructed by alternating layers of earth to form embankments, then filling the inner area, a technique characteristic of western Japan. Additionally, soil bags and clod stacking were used, a method typically reserved for high-status tombs in the late Kofun period. == Notes == {{Notelist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} * {{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳}} (Yotsukura, Iwaki) - Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. 112-meter keyhole-shaped tumulus. * {{ill|Ishikami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造}} [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:Fukushima Prefecture history]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[Category:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[Category:S11]] [[Category:Q--q]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:s11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:s11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[ja:塚前古墳]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:sukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[en:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] {{Kofun navbar}}

The mound has been significantly leveled and eroded over time.{{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} Excavations conducted by the Iwaki City Board of Education in fiscal 2016 (Heisei 28) and a survey by Fukushima University revealed its scale and construction method.{{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} The tumulus, now irregularly shaped, is reconstructed as originally being keyhole-shaped, with the front portion oriented southwest. Its mound measures 95–120 meters in length, ranking 3rd to 8th in size among Tōhoku kofun and is the largest from the late Kofun period in the region. Remains of haniwa, including cylindrical haniwa (some morning glory-shaped) and figurative haniwa (lid and shield-shaped), have been found on the mound surface. A surrounding moat (likely horseshoe-shaped) has also been identified. While the burial facilities are unknown, past records suggest the presence of ornaments such as gyōba (杏葉). Based on the haniwa and other artifacts, the mound's construction is estimated to date to the mid-6th century, making it significant for studying the late Kofun period in eastern Japan. == Site History == * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': Excavation due to residential development. Confirmed as a large-scale kofun (Iwaki City Board of Education). * '''2017 (Heisei 29)''': Survey conducted. Confirmed as the largest late Kofun in Tōhoku (Fukushima University Archaeological Research Laboratory).["Iwaki's Tsukamae Kofun: Largest Late Kofun in Tōhoku Revealed by Survey" (Mainichi Shimbun, May 11, 2017)]
["Tsukamae Kofun: Measuring 120m, Largest Late Tōhoku Kofun?" (Kahoku Shimpō, May 11, 2017)]. == Kofun Details == Measurements from the 2017 survey are as follows: * Mound length: 95–120 meters * Rear round portion: Diameter 53 meters * Front rectangular portion: ** Length: 54–70 meters ** Width: 45–65 meters The mound was constructed by alternating layers of earth to form embankments, then filling the inner area, a technique characteristic of western Japan. Additionally, soil bags and clod stacking were used, a method typically reserved for high-status tombs in the late Kofun period. == Notes == {{Notelist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} * {{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳}} (Yotsukura, Iwaki) - revision.text:
'''Tsukamae Kofun''' (塚前古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Onahama Rinjo|ja|小名浜林城}}, {{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|ja|いわき市}}, [[Fukushima Prefecture]]. Its shape is that of a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}. It is not designated as a historic site. Among kofun in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|ja|東北地方}}, it ranks 3rd to 8th in sizeMajor kofun in the Tōhoku region include: # {{ill|Raijin-yama Kofun|ja|雷神山古墳}} (Natori, Miyagi) - 168 meters in length. # {{ill|Kamegamori-Chinjumori Kofun|ja|亀ヶ森・鎮守森古墳}} (Aizubange, Fukushima) - 129 meters in length. # {{ill|Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|会津大塚山古墳}} (Aizuwakamatsu, Fukushima) - 114 meters in length. # {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳}} (Iwaki, Fukushima) - 112 meters in length. # {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (Sendai, Miyagi) - 110 meters in length. # {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳}} (Ōsaki, Miyagi) - 100 meters in length. # {{ill|Inarimori Kofun|ja|稲荷森古墳}} (Nanyō, Yamagata) - 96 meters in length. Recent surveys suggest that other kofun, including Tsukamae Kofun (95–120 meters in length), may also be classified as large-scale tumuli: * {{ill|Minamimori Kofun|ja|南森古墳}} (Nanyō, Yamagata) - Estimated to be 150–168 meters in length. and is the largest kofun from the late {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} in the Tōhoku region, dated to the mid-6th century. == Overview == Tsukamae Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus located in the eastern part of Fukushima Prefecture, in the Hamadōri area, at the head of Onahama Bay on the alluvial plain of the right bank of the Yada River (a tributary of the Fujiwara River). The mound has been significantly leveled and eroded over time.{{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} Excavations conducted by the Iwaki City Board of Education in fiscal 2016 (Heisei 28) and a survey by Fukushima University revealed its scale and construction method.{{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} The tumulus, now irregularly shaped, is reconstructed as originally being keyhole-shaped, with the front portion oriented southwest. Its mound measures 95–120 meters in length, ranking 3rd to 8th in size among Tōhoku kofun and is the largest from the late Kofun period in the region. Remains of haniwa, including cylindrical haniwa (some morning glory-shaped) and figurative haniwa (lid and shield-shaped), have been found on the mound surface. A surrounding moat (likely horseshoe-shaped) has also been identified. While the burial facilities are unknown, past records suggest the presence of ornaments such as gyōba (杏葉). Based on the haniwa and other artifacts, the mound's construction is estimated to date to the mid-6th century, making it significant for studying the late Kofun period in eastern Japan. == Site History == * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': Excavation due to residential development. Confirmed as a large-scale kofun (Iwaki City Board of Education). * '''2017 (Heisei 29)''': Survey conducted. Confirmed as the largest late Kofun in Tōhoku (Fukushima University Archaeological Research Laboratory).["Iwaki's Tsukamae Kofun: Largest Late Kofun in Tōhoku Revealed by Survey" (Mainichi Shimbun, May 11, 2017)]
["Tsukamae Kofun: Measuring 120m, Largest Late Tōhoku Kofun?" (Kahoku Shimpō, May 11, 2017)]. == Kofun Details == Measurements from the 2017 survey are as follows: * Mound length: 95–120 meters * Rear round portion: Diameter 53 meters * Front rectangular portion: ** Length: 54–70 meters ** Width: 45–65 meters The mound was constructed by alternating layers of earth to form embankments, then filling the inner area, a technique characteristic of western Japan. Additionally, soil bags and clod stacking were used, a method typically reserved for high-status tombs in the late Kofun period. == Notes == {{Notelist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} * {{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳}} (Yotsukura, Iwaki) - Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. 112-meter keyhole-shaped tumulus. * {{ill|Ishikami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:Fukushima Prefecture history]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:S11]] [[Category:Q--q]] [[Category:s11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[ja:塚前古墳]] [[sn:sukamae Kofun]] [[en:Tsukamae Kofun]]

Recent surveys suggest that other kofun, including Tsukamae Kofun (95–120 meters in length), may also be classified as large-scale tumuli: * {{ill|Minamimori Kofun|ja|南森古墳|12=draft}} (Nanyō, Yamagata) - Estimated to be 150–168 meters in length. and is the largest kofun from the late {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}} in the Tōhoku region, dated to the mid-6th century. == Overview == Tsukamae Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus located in the eastern part of Fukushima Prefecture, in the Hamadōri area, at the head of Onahama Bay on the alluvial plain of the right bank of the Yada River (a tributary of the Fujiwara River). The mound has been significantly leveled and eroded over time.{{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} Excavations conducted by the Iwaki City Board of Education in fiscal 2016 (Heisei 28) and a survey by Fukushima University revealed its scale and construction method.{{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} The tumulus, now irregularly shaped, is reconstructed as originally being keyhole-shaped, with the front portion oriented southwest. Its mound measures 95–120 meters in length, ranking 3rd to 8th in size among Tōhoku kofun and is the largest from the late Kofun period in the region. Remains of haniwa, including cylindrical haniwa (some morning glory-shaped) and figurative haniwa (lid and shield-shaped), have been found on the mound surface. A surrounding moat (likely horseshoe-shaped) has also been identified. While the burial facilities are unknown, past records suggest the presence of ornaments such as gyōba (杏葉). Based on the haniwa and other artifacts, the mound's construction is estimated to date to the mid-6th century, making it significant for studying the late Kofun period in eastern Japan. == Site History == * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': Excavation due to residential development. Confirmed as a large-scale kofun (Iwaki City Board of Education). * '''2017 (Heisei 29)''': Survey conducted. Confirmed as the largest late Kofun in Tōhoku (Fukushima University Archaeological Research Laboratory).["Iwaki's Tsukamae Kofun: Largest Late Kofun in Tōhoku Revealed by Survey" (Mainichi Shimbun, May 11, 2017)]
["Tsukamae Kofun: Measuring 120m, Largest Late Tōhoku Kofun?" (Kahoku Shimpō, May 11, 2017)]. == Kofun Details == Measurements from the 2017 survey are as follows: * Mound length: 95–120 meters * Rear round portion: Diameter 53 meters * Front rectangular portion: ** Length: 54–70 meters ** Width: 45–65 meters The mound was constructed by alternating layers of earth to form embankments, then filling the inner area, a technique characteristic of western Japan. Additionally, soil bags and clod stacking were used, a method typically reserved for high-status tombs in the late Kofun period. == Notes == {{Notelist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} * {{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|12=draft}} (Yotsukura, Iwaki) - Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. 112-meter keyhole-shaped tumulus. * {{ill|Ishikami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造|12=draft}} [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:qq]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:Fukushima Prefecture history]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:S11]] [[Category:Q--q]] [[Category:s11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]]{{Kofun navbar}}[[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[ja:塚前古墳]] [[sn:sukamae Kofun]] [[en:Tsukamae Kofun]]

Additionally, soil bags and clod stacking were used, a method typically reserved for high-status tombs in the late Kofun period. == Notes == {{Notelist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} * {{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|12=draft}} (Yotsukura, Iwaki) - Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. 112-meter keyhole-shaped tumulus. * {{ill|Ishikami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造|12=draft}} [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:qq]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:Fukushima Prefecture history]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:S11]] [[Category:Q--q]] [[Category:s11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[ja:塚前古墳]] [[sn:sukamae Kofun]] [[en:Tsukamae Kofun]] {{Kofun navbar}}

'''Tsukamae Kofun''' (塚前古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Onahama Rinjo|ja|小名浜林城|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|ja|いわき市|12=draft}}, [[Fukushima Prefecture]]. Its shape is that of a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft}}. It is not designated as a historic site. Among kofun in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|ja|東北地方|12=draft}}, it ranks 3rd to 8th in sizeMajor kofun in the Tōhoku region include: # {{ill|Raijin-yama Kofun|ja|雷神山古墳|12=draft}} (Natori, Miyagi) - 168 meters in length. # {{ill|Kamegamori-Chinjumori Kofun|ja|亀ヶ森・鎮守森古墳|12=draft}} (Aizubange, Fukushima) - 129 meters in length. # {{ill|Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|会津大塚山古墳|12=draft}} (Aizuwakamatsu, Fukushima) - 114 meters in length. # {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|12=draft}} (Iwaki, Fukushima) - 112 meters in length. # {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳|12=draft}} (Sendai, Miyagi) - 110 meters in length. # {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳|12=draft}} (Ōsaki, Miyagi) - 100 meters in length. # {{ill|Inarimori Kofun|ja|稲荷森古墳|12=draft}} (Nanyō, Yamagata) - 96 meters in length. Recent surveys suggest that other kofun, including Tsukamae Kofun (95–120 meters in length), may also be classified as large-scale tumuli: * {{ill|Minamimori Kofun|ja|南森古墳|12=draft}} (Nanyō, Yamagata) - Estimated to be 150–168 meters in length. and is the largest kofun from the late {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}} in the Tōhoku region, dated to the mid-6th century. == Overview == Tsukamae Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus located in the eastern part of Fukushima Prefecture, in the Hamadōri area, at the head of Onahama Bay on the alluvial plain of the right bank of the Yada River (a tributary of the Fujiwara River). The mound has been significantly leveled and eroded over time.{{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} Excavations conducted by the Iwaki City Board of Education in fiscal 2016 (Heisei 28) and a survey by Fukushima University revealed its scale and construction method.{{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} The tumulus, now irregularly shaped, is reconstructed as originally being keyhole-shaped, with the front portion oriented southwest. Its mound measures 95–120 meters in length, ranking 3rd to 8th in size among Tōhoku kofun and is the largest from the late Kofun period in the region. Remains of haniwa, including cylindrical haniwa (some morning glory-shaped) and figurative haniwa (lid and shield-shaped), have been found on the mound surface. A surrounding moat (likely horseshoe-shaped) has also been identified. While the burial facilities are unknown, past records suggest the presence of ornaments such as gyōba (杏葉). Based on the haniwa and other artifacts, the mound's construction is estimated to date to the mid-6th century, making it significant for studying the late Kofun period in eastern Japan. == Site History == * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': Excavation due to residential development. Confirmed as a large-scale kofun (Iwaki City Board of Education). * '''2017 (Heisei 29)''': Survey conducted. Confirmed as the largest late Kofun in Tōhoku (Fukushima University Archaeological Research Laboratory).["Iwaki's Tsukamae Kofun: Largest Late Kofun in Tōhoku Revealed by Survey" (Mainichi Shimbun, May 11, 2017)]
["Tsukamae Kofun: Measuring 120m, Largest Late Tōhoku Kofun?" (Kahoku Shimpō, May 11, 2017)]. == Kofun Details == Measurements from the 2017 survey are as follows: * Mound length: 95–120 meters * Rear round portion: Diameter 53 meters * Front rectangular portion: ** Length: 54–70 meters ** Width: 45–65 meters The mound was constructed by alternating layers of earth to form embankments, then filling the inner area, a technique characteristic of western Japan. Additionally, soil bags and clod stacking were used, a method typically reserved for high-status tombs in the late Kofun period. == Notes == {{Notelist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} * {{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|12=draft}} (Yotsukura, Iwaki) - Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. 112-meter keyhole-shaped tumulus. * {{ill|Ishikami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造|12=draft}} [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:qq]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:Fukushima Prefecture history]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:S11]] [[Category:s11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[ja:塚前古墳]] [[sn:sukamae Kofun]] [[en:Tsukamae Kofun]] {{Kofun navbar}}

Recent surveys suggest that other kofun, including Tsukamae Kofun (95–120 meters in length), may also be classified as large-scale tumuli: * {{ill|Minamimori Kofun|ja|南森古墳|12=draft}} (Nanyō, Yamagata) - Estimated to be 150–168 meters in length. and is the largest kofun from the late {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}} in the Tōhoku region, dated to the mid-6th century. == Overview == Tsukamae Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus located in the eastern part of Fukushima Prefecture, in the Hamadōri area, at the head of Onahama Bay on the alluvial plain of the right bank of the Yada River (a tributary of the Fujiwara River). The mound has been significantly leveled and eroded over time.{{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} Excavations conducted by the Iwaki City Board of Education in fiscal 2016 (Heisei 28) and a survey by Fukushima University revealed its scale and construction method.{{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} The tumulus, now irregularly shaped, is reconstructed as originally being keyhole-shaped, with the front portion oriented southwest. Its mound measures 95–120 meters in length, ranking 3rd to 8th in size among Tōhoku kofun and is the largest from the late Kofun period in the region. Remains of haniwa, including cylindrical haniwa (some morning glory-shaped) and figurative haniwa (lid and shield-shaped), have been found on the mound surface. A surrounding moat (likely horseshoe-shaped) has also been identified. While the burial facilities are unknown, past records suggest the presence of ornaments such as gyōba (杏葉). Based on the haniwa and other artifacts, the mound's construction is estimated to date to the mid-6th century, making it significant for studying the late Kofun period in eastern Japan. == Site History == * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': Excavation due to residential development. Confirmed as a large-scale kofun (Iwaki City Board of Education). * '''2017 (Heisei 29)''': Survey conducted. Confirmed as the largest late Kofun in Tōhoku (Fukushima University Archaeological Research Laboratory).["Iwaki's Tsukamae Kofun: Largest Late Kofun in Tōhoku Revealed by Survey" (Mainichi Shimbun, May 11, 2017)]
["Tsukamae Kofun: Measuring 120m, Largest Late Tōhoku Kofun?" (Kahoku Shimpō, May 11, 2017)]. == Kofun Details == Measurements from the 2017 survey are as follows: * Mound length: 95–120 meters * Rear round portion: Diameter 53 meters * Front rectangular portion: ** Length: 54–70 meters ** Width: 45–65 meters The mound was constructed by alternating layers of earth to form embankments, then filling the inner area, a technique characteristic of western Japan. Additionally, soil bags and clod stacking were used, a method typically reserved for high-status tombs in the late Kofun period. == Notes == {{Notelist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} * {{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|12=draft}} (Yotsukura, Iwaki) - Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. 112-meter keyhole-shaped tumulus. * {{ill|Ishikami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造|12=draft}} [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:qq]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:Fukushima Prefecture history]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[ja:塚前古墳]] [[sn:sukamae Kofun]] [[en:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] {{Kofun navbar}}

Among kofun in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|ja|東北地方|12=draft}}, it ranks 3rd to 8th in sizeMajor kofun in the Tōhoku region include: # {{ill|Raijin-yama Kofun|ja|雷神山古墳|12=draft}} (Natori, Miyagi) - 168 meters in length. # {{ill|Kamegamori-Chinjumori Kofun|ja|亀ヶ森・鎮守森古墳|12=draft}} (Aizubange, Fukushima) - 129 meters in length. # {{ill|Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|会津大塚山古墳|12=draft}} (Aizuwakamatsu, Fukushima) - 114 meters in length. # {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|12=draft}} (Iwaki, Fukushima) - 112 meters in length. # {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳|12=draft}} (Sendai, Miyagi) - 110 meters in length. # {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳|12=draft}} (Ōsaki, Miyagi) - 100 meters in length. # {{ill|Inarimori Kofun|ja|稲荷森古墳|12=draft}} (Nanyō, Yamagata) - 96 meters in length. The mound has been significantly leveled and eroded over time.{{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} Excavations conducted by the Iwaki City Board of Education in fiscal 2016 (Heisei 28) and a survey by Fukushima University revealed its scale and construction method.{{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} The tumulus, now irregularly shaped, is reconstructed as originally being keyhole-shaped, with the front portion oriented southwest. Its mound measures 95–120 meters in length, ranking 3rd to 8th in size among Tōhoku kofun and is the largest from the late Kofun period in the region. Remains of haniwa, including cylindrical haniwa (some morning glory-shaped) and figurative haniwa (lid and shield-shaped), have been found on the mound surface. A surrounding moat (likely horseshoe-shaped) has also been identified. While the burial facilities are unknown, past records suggest the presence of ornaments such as gyōba (杏葉). Based on the haniwa and other artifacts, the mound's construction is estimated to date to the mid-6th century, making it significant for studying the late Kofun period in eastern Japan. == Site History == * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': Excavation due to residential development. Confirmed as a large-scale kofun (Iwaki City Board of Education). * '''2017 (Heisei 29)''': Survey conducted. Confirmed as the largest late Kofun in Tōhoku (Fukushima University Archaeological Research Laboratory).["Iwaki's Tsukamae Kofun: Largest Late Kofun in Tōhoku Revealed by Survey" (Mainichi Shimbun, May 11, 2017)]
["Tsukamae Kofun: Measuring 120m, Largest Late Tōhoku Kofun?" (Kahoku Shimpō, May 11, 2017)]. == Kofun Details == Measurements from the 2017 survey are as follows: * Mound length: 95–120 meters * Rear round portion: Diameter 53 meters * Front rectangular portion: ** Length: 54–70 meters ** Width: 45–65 meters The mound was constructed by alternating layers of earth to form embankments, then filling the inner area, a technique characteristic of western Japan. Additionally, soil bags and clod stacking were used, a method typically reserved for high-status tombs in the late Kofun period. == Notes == {{Notelist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} * {{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|12=draft}} (Yotsukura, Iwaki) - Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. 112-meter keyhole-shaped tumulus. * {{ill|Ishikami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造|12=draft}} [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:qq]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:Fukushima Prefecture history]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[ja:塚前古墳]] [[sn:sukamae Kofun]] [[en:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] {{Kofun navbar}}

Its shape is that of a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is not designated as a historic site. Among kofun in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|ja|東北地方|12=draft|12=draft}}, it ranks 3rd to 8th in sizeMajor kofun in the Tōhoku region include: # {{ill|Raijin-yama Kofun|ja|雷神山古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} (Natori, Miyagi) - 168 meters in length. # {{ill|Kamegamori-Chinjumori Kofun|ja|亀ヶ森・鎮守森古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} (Aizubange, Fukushima) - 129 meters in length. # {{ill|Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|会津大塚山古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} (Aizuwakamatsu, Fukushima) - 114 meters in length. # {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} (Iwaki, Fukushima) - 112 meters in length. # {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} (Sendai, Miyagi) - 110 meters in length. # {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} (Ōsaki, Miyagi) - 100 meters in length. # {{ill|Inarimori Kofun|ja|稲荷森古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} (Nanyō, Yamagata) - 96 meters in length. Recent surveys suggest that other kofun, including Tsukamae Kofun (95–120 meters in length), may also be classified as large-scale tumuli: * {{ill|Minamimori Kofun|ja|南森古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} (Nanyō, Yamagata) - Estimated to be 150–168 meters in length. and is the largest kofun from the late {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft|12=draft}} in the Tōhoku region, dated to the mid-6th century. == Overview == Tsukamae Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus located in the eastern part of Fukushima Prefecture, in the Hamadōri area, at the head of Onahama Bay on the alluvial plain of the right bank of the Yada River (a tributary of the Fujiwara River). The mound has been significantly leveled and eroded over time.{{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} Excavations conducted by the Iwaki City Board of Education in fiscal 2016 (Heisei 28) and a survey by Fukushima University revealed its scale and construction method.{{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} The tumulus, now irregularly shaped, is reconstructed as originally being keyhole-shaped, with the front portion oriented southwest. Its mound measures 95–120 meters in length, ranking 3rd to 8th in size among Tōhoku kofun and is the largest from the late Kofun period in the region. Remains of haniwa, including cylindrical haniwa (some morning glory-shaped) and figurative haniwa (lid and shield-shaped), have been found on the mound surface. A surrounding moat (likely horseshoe-shaped) has also been identified. While the burial facilities are unknown, past records suggest the presence of ornaments such as gyōba (杏葉). Based on the haniwa and other artifacts, the mound's construction is estimated to date to the mid-6th century, making it significant for studying the late Kofun period in eastern Japan. == Site History == * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': Excavation due to residential development. Confirmed as a large-scale kofun (Iwaki City Board of Education). * '''2017 (Heisei 29)''': Survey conducted. Confirmed as the largest late Kofun in Tōhoku (Fukushima University Archaeological Research Laboratory).["Iwaki's Tsukamae Kofun: Largest Late Kofun in Tōhoku Revealed by Survey" (Mainichi Shimbun, May 11, 2017)]
["Tsukamae Kofun: Measuring 120m, Largest Late Tōhoku Kofun?" (Kahoku Shimpō, May 11, 2017)]. == Kofun Details == Measurements from the 2017 survey are as follows: * Mound length: 95–120 meters * Rear round portion: Diameter 53 meters * Front rectangular portion: ** Length: 54–70 meters ** Width: 45–65 meters The mound was constructed by alternating layers of earth to form embankments, then filling the inner area, a technique characteristic of western Japan. Additionally, soil bags and clod stacking were used, a method typically reserved for high-status tombs in the late Kofun period. == Notes == {{Notelist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} * {{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} (Yotsukura, Iwaki) - Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. 112-meter keyhole-shaped tumulus. * {{ill|Ishikami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造|12=draft|12=draft}} {{draft categories| }} {{Kofun navbar}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:Fukushima Prefecture history]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[ja:塚前古墳]] [[sn:sukamae Kofun]] [[en:Tsukamae Kofun]]

["Tsukamae Kofun: Measuring 120m, Largest Late Tōhoku Kofun?" (Kahoku Shimpō, May 11, 2017)]. == Kofun Details == Measurements from the 2017 survey are as follows: * Mound length: 95–120 meters * Rear round portion: Diameter 53 meters * Front rectangular portion: ** Length: 54–70 meters ** Width: 45–65 meters The mound was constructed by alternating layers of earth to form embankments, then filling the inner area, a technique characteristic of western Japan. Additionally, soil bags and clod stacking were used, a method typically reserved for high-status tombs in the late Kofun period. == Notes == {{Notelist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} * {{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Yotsukura, Iwaki) - Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. 112-meter keyhole-shaped tumulus. * {{ill|Ishikami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{draft categories| }} {{Kofun navbar}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:Fukushima Prefecture history]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[ja:塚前古墳]] [[sn:sukamae Kofun]] [[en:Tsukamae Kofun]]

revision.comment: 18 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:塚前古墳 up to rev 99995314
revision.text:
{{日本の古墳 |名称 = 塚前古墳 |画像 = [[File:Tsukamae burial mound Iwaki Fukushima.jpg|250px|外観(2022年4月)]] |別名 = |所属 = |所在地 = [[福島県]][[いわき市]]小名浜林城字塚前 |緯度度 = 36|緯度分 = 59|緯度秒 = 7.56 |経度度 =140|経度分 = 53|経度秒 = 43.74 |ISO = JP-07 |形状 = [[前方後円墳]] |規模 = 墳丘長95-120m |築造年代 = [[6世紀]]中頃 |埋葬施設 = 不明 |被葬者 = |出土品 = [[埴輪]]片・土器片 |史跡指定 = なし |特記事項 = [[東北地方]]第3-8位の規模 |地図2 = {{Location map+|Japan Fukushima|width=220|float=center|caption=|places= {{Location map~|Japan Fukushima|lat_deg=37|lat_min=35|lat_sec=34.89|lon_deg=139|lon_min=49|lon_sec=44.77|position=top|mark=前方後円墳-西北西.svg|marksize=24|label=[[亀ヶ森・鎮守森古墳|亀ヶ森古墳]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Fukushima|lat_deg=37|lat_min=30|lat_sec=46.83|lon_deg=139|lon_min=56|lon_sec=38.84|position=bottom|mark=前方後円墳-北北西.svg|marksize=24|label=[[会津大塚山古墳|会津大塚山
古墳]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Fukushima|lat_deg=37|lat_min=6|lat_sec=53.98|lon_deg=140|lon_min=56|lon_sec=37.78|position=left|mark=前方後円墳-西.svg|marksize=24|label=[[玉山古墳]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Fukushima|lat_deg=36|lat_min=59|lat_sec=7.56|lon_deg=140|lon_min=53|lon_sec=43.74|position=left|mark=前方後円墳-西南西.svg|marksize=24|label='''塚前古墳'''}}}} }} '''塚前古墳'''（つかまえこふん）は、[[福島県]][[いわき市]]小名浜林城（りんじょう）にある[[古墳]]。形状は[[前方後円墳]]。史跡指定はされていない。 [[東北地方]]では第3-8位東北地方における主な古墳は次の通り。 # [[雷神山古墳]]（宮城県名取市） - 墳丘長168[[メートル]]。 # [[亀ヶ森・鎮守森古墳#亀ヶ森古墳|亀ヶ森古墳]]（福島県河沼郡会津坂下町） - 墳丘長129メートル。 # [[会津大塚山古墳]]（福島県会津若松市） - 墳丘長114メートル。 # [[玉山古墳]]（福島県いわき市） - 墳丘長112メートル。 # [[遠見塚古墳]]（宮城県仙台市） - 墳丘長110メートル。 # 青塚古墳（宮城県大崎市） - 墳丘長100メートル。 # [[稲荷森古墳]]（山形県南陽市） - 墳丘長96メートル。 また近年の調査では、規模全容は未判明ながら、塚前古墳（墳丘長95-120メートル）のほか次の古墳が大型古墳と推定される。 * [[南森古墳]]（山形県南陽市） - 墳丘長150-168メートル。、また[[古墳時代]]後期としては東北地方で最大規模の古墳で、[[6世紀]]中頃の築造と推定される。 == 概要 == 福島県東部の[[浜通り|浜通り地方]]、小名浜湾奥部で矢田川（藤原川支流）右岸の沖積低地上に築造された大型前方後円墳である{{Sfn|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}}。これまでに墳丘は削平を受けて大きく崩れているほか{{Sfn|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}}、[[2016年]]度（[[平成]]28年度）には発掘調査（いわき市教育委員会）および測量調査（[[福島大学]]）が実施されている{{Sfn|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}}{{Sfn|福島大学 測量調査|2017}}。 墳形は現在では不整形をなすが{{Sfn|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}}、元は前方後円形と復元され、前方部を南西方に向ける{{Sfn|福島大学 測量調査|2017}}。墳丘長は95-120メートルを測り、[[東北地方]]では第3-8位の規模になるとともに、後期古墳としては東北地方で最大規模になる{{Sfn|福島大学 測量調査|2017}}。墳丘外表では[[埴輪]]片として[[円筒埴輪]]（朝顔形埴輪含む）・[[形象埴輪]]（蓋形・盾形埴輪）が検出されているほか、墳丘周囲では周溝（馬蹄形か）の存在が認められる{{Sfn|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}}。埋葬施設は明らかでない{{Sfn|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}}。出土品としては埴輪のほかに[[土師器]]・[[弥生土器]]が認められ、かつては杏葉の出土があったとも伝わる{{Sfn|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}}。 この塚前古墳は、出土埴輪等より[[古墳時代]]後期の[[6世紀]]中頃の築造と推定される{{Sfn|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}}。規模の点とともに墳丘盛土の構築手法の点で特色を有しており、東国の後期古墳を考察するうえで重要視される古墳になる{{Sfn|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}}。 == 遺跡歴 == * [[2016年]]（[[平成]]28年）10-11月、宅地造成工事に伴う発掘調査。大規模古墳と判明（いわき市教育委員会）{{Sfn|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}}。 * [[2017年]]（平成29年）3月、測量調査。後期古墳としては東北地方最大規模と判明（[[福島大学]]考古学研究室）{{Sfn|福島大学 測量調査|2017}}[https://mainichi.jp/articles/20170511/ddl/k07/040/020000c "いわき・塚前古墳 東北最大の後期古墳 大和政権とのつながり示す 福島大調査／福島"]（毎日新聞、2017年5月11日記事）。
[http://www.kahoku.co.jp/tohokunews/201705/20170511_63011.html "塚前古墳 全長120m 後期東北最大か"]（河北新報、2017年5月11日記事）。。 == 墳丘 == 墳丘の規模は次の通り（2017年（平成29年）の測量調査値）{{Sfn|福島大学 測量調査|2017}}。 * 墳丘長：95-120メートル * 後円部 ** 直径：53メートル * 前方部 ** 長さ：54-70メートル ** 幅：45-65メートル 墳丘は、外縁部に盛土を土手状に積み上げたのちに内側を土手上端まで盛り上げる、という工程の繰り返しで構築されるが、これは西日本によく見られる手法（西日本的工法）になる{{Sfn|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}}。また、盛土に際しては土嚢・土塊積みの採用が認められるが、これは後期古墳では有力者の墓に特化した手法とされる{{Sfn|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}}。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''注釈''' {{Reflist|group="注"}} '''出典''' {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == {{small|（記事執筆に使用した文献）}} * {{Wikicite|reference={{PDFlink|[http://www.fukushima-u.ac.jp/press/H29/pdf/101-03.pdf 「いわき市 塚前古墳測量調査とその成果」]}} 福島大学行政政策学類考古学研究室、2017年5月10日定例記者会見資料 |ref={{Harvid|福島大学 測量調査|2017}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=公益財団法人いわき市教育文化事業団編|author=|year=2017|chapter=|title=塚前古墳 -藤原川流域における後期前方後円墳の調査概報-|publisher=いわき市教育委員会|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}}}} == 関連文献 == {{small|（記事執筆に使用していない関連文献）}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=福島大学行政政策学類, 福島大学行政政策学類考古学研究室 |title=団子山古墳5 : 福島県須賀川市団子山古墳第7次調査報告書 ; 塚前古墳1 : 福島県いわき市塚前古墳測量調査報告書 |publisher=福島大学行政政策学類 : 福島大学行政政策学類考古学研究室 |year=2018 |series=福島大学考古学研究報告 |pages=1-62 |NCID=BB25998732 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10270/4871 |ref={{Harvid|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}}}} == 関連項目 == * [[玉山古墳]]（いわき市四倉町玉山） - 福島県指定史跡。墳丘長112メートルの前方後円墳。 * [[石城国造]] {{DEFAULTSORT:つかまえこふん}} [[Category:福島県の古墳]] [[Category:いわき市の歴史]] [[Category:前方後円墳]]

[http://www.kahoku.co.jp/tohokunews/201705/20170511_63011.html "塚前古墳 全長120m 後期東北最大か"]（河北新報、2017年5月11日記事）。。 == 墳丘 == 墳丘の規模は次の通り（2017年（平成29年）の測量調査値）{{Sfn|福島大学 測量調査|2017}}。 * 墳丘長：95-120メートル * 後円部 ** 直径：53メートル * 前方部 ** 長さ：54-70メートル ** 幅：45-65メートル 墳丘は、外縁部に盛土を土手状に積み上げたのちに内側を土手上端まで盛り上げる、という工程の繰り返しで構築されるが、これは西日本によく見られる手法（西日本的工法）になる{{Sfn|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}}。また、盛土に際しては土嚢・土塊積みの採用が認められるが、これは後期古墳では有力者の墓に特化した手法とされる{{Sfn|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}}。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''注釈''' {{Reflist|group="注"}} '''出典''' {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == {{small|（記事執筆に使用した文献）}} * {{Wikicite|reference={{PDFlink|[http://www.fukushima-u.ac.jp/press/H29/pdf/101-03.pdf 「いわき市 塚前古墳測量調査とその成果」]}} 福島大学行政政策学類考古学研究室、2017年5月10日定例記者会見資料 |ref={{Harvid|福島大学 測量調査|2017}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=公益財団法人いわき市教育文化事業団編|author=|year=2017|chapter=|title=塚前古墳 -藤原川流域における後期前方後円墳の調査概報-|publisher=いわき市教育委員会|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}}}} == 関連文献 == {{small|（記事執筆に使用していない関連文献）}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=福島大学行政政策学類, 福島大学行政政策学類考古学研究室 |title=団子山古墳5 : 福島県須賀川市団子山古墳第7次調査報告書 ; 塚前古墳1 : 福島県いわき市塚前古墳測量調査報告書 |publisher=福島大学行政政策学類 : 福島大学行政政策学類考古学研究室 |year=2018 |series=福島大学考古学研究報告 |pages=1-62 |NCID=BB25998732 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10270/4871 |ref={{Harvid|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}}}} == 関連項目 == * [[玉山古墳]]（いわき市四倉町玉山） - 福島県指定史跡。墳丘長112メートルの前方後円墳。 * [[石城国造]] {{DEFAULTSORT:つかまえこふん}} [[Category:福島県の古墳]] [[Category:いわき市の歴史]] [[Category:前方後円墳]]

'''Tsukamae Kofun''' (塚前古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Onahama Rinjo|ja|小名浜林城|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|ja|いわき市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, [[Fukushima Prefecture]]. Its shape is that of a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is not designated as a historic site. Among kofun in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|ja|東北地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it Ranks 3rd to 8th in sizeMajor kofun in the Tōhoku region include: # {{ill|Raijin-yama Kofun|ja|雷神山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Natori, Miyagi) - 168 meters in length. # {{ill|Kamegamori-Chinjumori Kofun|ja|亀ヶ森・鎮守森古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Aizubange, Fukushima) - 129 meters in length. # {{ill|Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|会津大塚山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Aizuwakamatsu, Fukushima) - 114 meters in length. # {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Iwaki, Fukushima) - 112 meters in length. # {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Sendai, Miyagi) - 110 meters in length. # {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Ōsaki, Miyagi) - 100 meters in length. # {{ill|Inarimori Kofun|ja|稲荷森古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Nanyō, Yamagata) - 96 meters in length. Recent surveys suggest that other kofun, including Tsukamae Kofun (95–120 meters in length), may also be classified as large-scale tumuli: * {{ill|Minamimori Kofun|ja|南森古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Nanyō, Yamagata) - Estimated to be 150–168 meters in length. and is the largest kofun from the late {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the Tōhoku region, dated to the mid-6th century. == Overview == Tsukamae Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus located in the eastern part of Fukushima Prefecture, in the Hamadōri area, at the head of Onahama Bay on the alluvial plain of the right bank of the Yada River (a tributary of the Fujiwara River). The mound has been significantly leveled and eroded over time.{{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} Excavations conducted by the Iwaki City Board of Education in fiscal 2016 (Heisei 28) and a survey by Fukushima University revealed its scale and construction method.{{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} The tumulus, now irregularly shaped, is reconstructed as originally being keyhole-shaped, with the front portion oriented southwest. Its mound measures 95–120 meters in length, Ranking 3rd to 8th in size among Tōhoku kofun and is the largest from the late Kofun period in the region. Remains of haniwa, including cylindrical haniwa (some morning glory-shaped) and figurative haniwa (lid and shield-shaped), have been found on the mound surface. A surrounding moat (likely horseshoe-shaped) has also been identified. While the burial facilities are unknown, past records suggest the presence of ornaments such as gyōba (杏葉). Based on the haniwa and other artifacts, the mound's construction is estimated to date to the mid-6th century, making it significant for studying the late Kofun period in eastern Japan. == Site History == * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': Excavation due to residential development. Confirmed as a large-scale kofun (Iwaki City Board of Education). * '''2017 (Heisei 29)''': Survey conducted. Confirmed as the largest late Kofun in Tōhoku (Fukushima University Archaeological Research Laboratory).["Iwaki's Tsukamae Kofun: Largest Late Kofun in Tōhoku Revealed by Survey" (Mainichi Shimbun, May 11, 2017)]
["Tsukamae Kofun: Measuring 120m, Largest Late Tōhoku Kofun?" (Kahoku Shimpō, May 11, 2017)]. == Kofun Details == Measurements from the 2017 survey are as follows: * Mound length: 95–120 meters * Rear round portion: Diameter 53 meters * Front rectangular portion: ** Length: 54–70 meters ** Width: 45–65 meters The mound was constructed by alternating layers of earth to form embankments, then filling the inner area, a technique characteristic of western Japan. Additionally, soil bags and clod stacking were used, a method typically reserved for high-status tombs in the late Kofun period. == Notes == {{Notelist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} * {{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Yotsukura, Iwaki) - Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. 112-meter keyhole-shaped tumulus. * {{ill|Ishikami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{draft categories| }} {{Kofun navbar}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:Fukushima Prefecture history]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[ja:塚前古墳]] [[sn:sukamae Kofun]] [[en:Tsukamae Kofun]] {{translated page|ja|塚前古墳|version=99995314|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Confirmed as the largest late Kofun in Tōhoku (Fukushima University Archaeological Research Laboratory).["Iwaki's Tsukamae Kofun: Largest Late Kofun in Tōhoku Revealed by Survey" (Mainichi Shimbun, May 11, 2017)]
["Tsukamae Kofun: Measuring 120m, Largest Late Tōhoku Kofun?" (Kahoku Shimpō, May 11, 2017)]. == Kofun Details == Measurements from the 2017 survey are as follows: * Mound length: 95–120 meters * Rear round portion: Diameter 53 meters * Front rectangular portion: ** Length: 54–70 meters ** Width: 45–65 meters The mound was constructed by alternating layers of earth to form embankments, then filling the inner area, a technique characteristic of western Japan. Additionally, soil bags and clod stacking were used, a method typically reserved for high-status tombs in the late Kofun period. == Notes == {{Notelist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} * {{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Yotsukura, Iwaki) - Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. 112-meter keyhole-shaped tumulus. * {{ill|Ishikami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|塚前古墳|version=99995314|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:Fukushima Prefecture history]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]]}} [[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[ja:塚前古墳]] [[sn:sukamae Kofun]] [[en:Tsukamae Kofun]]

revision.text:
'''Tsukamae Kofun''' (塚前古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Onahama Rinjo|ja|小名浜林城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onahama Rinjo|qq=}}, {{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|ja|いわき市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwaki, Fukushima|qq=}}, [[Fukushima Prefecture]]. Its shape is that of a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped tumulus|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}}. It is not designated as a historic site. Among kofun in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|ja|東北地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōhoku region|qq=}}, it Ranks 3rd to 8th in sizeMajor kofun in the Tōhoku region include: # {{ill|Raijin-yama Kofun|ja|雷神山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raijin-yama Kofun|qq=}} (Natori, Miyagi) - 168 meters in length. # {{ill|Kamegamori-Chinjumori Kofun|ja|亀ヶ森・鎮守森古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamegamori-Chinjumori Kofun|qq=}} (Aizubange, Fukushima) - 129 meters in length. # {{ill|Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|会津大塚山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|qq=}} (Aizuwakamatsu, Fukushima) - 114 meters in length. # {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayama Kofun|qq=}} (Iwaki, Fukushima) - 112 meters in length. # {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōmizuka Kofun|qq=}} (Sendai, Miyagi) - 110 meters in length. # {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aotsuka Kofun|qq=}} (Ōsaki, Miyagi) - 100 meters in length. # {{ill|Inarimori Kofun|ja|稲荷森古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inarimori Kofun|qq=}} (Nanyō, Yamagata) - 96 meters in length. Recent surveys suggest that other kofun, including Tsukamae Kofun (95–120 meters in length), may also be classified as large-scale tumuli: * {{ill|Minamimori Kofun|ja|南森古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamimori Kofun|qq=}} (Nanyō, Yamagata) - Estimated to be 150–168 meters in length. and is the largest kofun from the late {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period|qq=}} in the Tōhoku region, dated to the mid-6th century. == Overview == Tsukamae Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus located in the eastern part of Fukushima Prefecture, in the Hamadōri area, at the head of Onahama Bay on the alluvial plain of the right bank of the Yada River (a tributary of the Fujiwara River). The mound has been significantly leveled and eroded over time.{{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} Excavations conducted by the Iwaki City Board of Education in fiscal 2016 (Heisei 28) and a survey by Fukushima University revealed its scale and construction method.{{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} The tumulus, now irregularly shaped, is reconstructed as originally being keyhole-shaped, with the front portion oriented southwest. Its mound measures 95–120 meters in length, Ranking 3rd to 8th in size among Tōhoku kofun and is the largest from the late Kofun period in the region. Remains of haniwa, including cylindrical haniwa (some morning glory-shaped) and figurative haniwa (lid and shield-shaped), have been found on the mound surface. A surrounding moat (likely horseshoe-shaped) has also been identified. While the burial facilities are unknown, past records suggest the presence of ornaments such as gyōba (杏葉). Based on the haniwa and other artifacts, the mound's construction is estimated to date to the mid-6th century, making it significant for studying the late Kofun period in eastern Japan. == Site History == * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': Excavation due to residential development. Confirmed as a large-scale kofun (Iwaki City Board of Education). * '''2017 (Heisei 29)''': Survey conducted. Confirmed as the largest late Kofun in Tōhoku (Fukushima University Archaeological Research Laboratory).["Iwaki's Tsukamae Kofun: Largest Late Kofun in Tōhoku Revealed by Survey" (Mainichi Shimbun, May 11, 2017)]
["Tsukamae Kofun: Measuring 120m, Largest Late Tōhoku Kofun?" (Kahoku Shimpō, May 11, 2017)]. == Kofun Details == Measurements from the 2017 survey are as follows: * Mound length: 95–120 meters * Rear round portion: Diameter 53 meters * Front rectangular portion: ** Length: 54–70 meters ** Width: 45–65 meters The mound was constructed by alternating layers of earth to form embankments, then filling the inner area, a technique characteristic of western Japan. Additionally, soil bags and clod stacking were used, a method typically reserved for high-status tombs in the late Kofun period. == Notes == {{Notelist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} * {{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayama Kofun|qq=}} (Yotsukura, Iwaki) - Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. 112-meter keyhole-shaped tumulus. * {{ill|Ishikami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|qq=}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|塚前古墳|version=99995314|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:Fukushima Prefecture history]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]]}} [[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[ja:塚前古墳]] [[sn:sukamae Kofun]] [[en:Tsukamae Kofun]]

Among kofun in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|ja|東北地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōhoku region|qq=}}, it Ranks 3rd to 8th in sizeMajor kofun in the Tōhoku region include: # {{ill|Raijin-yama Kofun|ja|雷神山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raijin-yama Kofun|qq=}} (Natori, Miyagi) - 168 meters in length. # {{ill|Kamegamori-Chinjumori Kofun|ja|亀ヶ森・鎮守森古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamegamori-Chinjumori Kofun|qq=}} (Aizubange, Fukushima) - 129 meters in length. # {{ill|Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|会津大塚山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|qq=}} (Aizuwakamatsu, Fukushima) - 114 meters in length. # {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayama Kofun|qq=}} (Iwaki, Fukushima) - 112 meters in length. # {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōmizuka Kofun|qq=}} (Sendai, Miyagi) - 110 meters in length. # {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aotsuka Kofun|qq=}} (Ōsaki, Miyagi) - 100 meters in length. # {{ill|Inarimori Kofun|ja|稲荷森古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inarimori Kofun|qq=}} (Nanyō, Yamagata) - 96 meters in length. Recent surveys suggest that other kofun, including Tsukamae Kofun (95–120 meters in length), may also be classified as large-scale tumuli: * {{ill|Minamimori Kofun|ja|南森古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamimori Kofun|qq=}} (Nanyō, Yamagata) - Estimated to be 150–168 meters in length. and is the largest kofun from the late {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period|qq=}} in the Tōhoku region, dated to the mid-6th century. == Overview == Tsukamae Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus located in the eastern part of Fukushima Prefecture, in the Hamadōri area, at the head of Onahama Bay on the alluvial plain of the right bank of the Yada River (a tributary of the Fujiwara River). The mound has been significantly leveled and eroded over time.{{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} Excavations conducted by the Iwaki City Board of Education in fiscal 2016 (Heisei 28) and a survey by Fukushima University revealed its scale and construction method.{{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} The tumulus, now irregularly shaped, is reconstructed as originally being keyhole-shaped, with the front portion oriented southwest. Its mound measures 95–120 meters in length, Ranking 3rd to 8th in size among Tōhoku kofun and is the largest from the late Kofun period in the region. Remains of haniwa, including cylindrical haniwa (some morning glory-shaped) and figurative haniwa (lid and shield-shaped), have been found on the mound surface. A surrounding moat (likely horseshoe-shaped) has also been identified. While the burial facilities are unknown, past records suggest the presence of ornaments such as gyōba (杏葉). Based on the haniwa and other artifacts, the mound's construction is estimated to date to the mid-6th century, making it significant for studying the late Kofun period in eastern Japan. == Site History == * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': Excavation due to residential development. Confirmed as a large-scale kofun (Iwaki City Board of Education). * '''2017 (Heisei 29)''': Survey conducted. Confirmed as the largest late Kofun in Tōhoku (Fukushima University Archaeological Research Laboratory).["Iwaki's Tsukamae Kofun: Largest Late Kofun in Tōhoku Revealed by Survey" (Mainichi Shimbun, May 11, 2017)]
["Tsukamae Kofun: Measuring 120m, Largest Late Tōhoku Kofun?" (Kahoku Shimpō, May 11, 2017)]. == Kofun Details == Measurements from the 2017 survey are as follows: * Mound length: 95–120 meters * Rear round portion: Diameter 53 meters * Front rectangular portion: ** Length: 54–70 meters ** Width: 45–65 meters The mound was constructed by alternating layers of earth to form embankments, then filling the inner area, a technique characteristic of western Japan. Additionally, soil bags and clod stacking were used, a method typically reserved for high-status tombs in the late Kofun period. == Notes == {{Notelist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} * {{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayama Kofun|qq=}} (Yotsukura, Iwaki) - Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. 112-meter keyhole-shaped tumulus. * {{ill|Ishikami no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Ishikami no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Ishikami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|qq=}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|塚前古墳|version=99995314|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:Fukushima Prefecture history]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]]}} [[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[ja:塚前古墳]] [[sn:sukamae Kofun]] [[en:Tsukamae Kofun]]

Recent surveys suggest that other kofun, including Tsukamae Kofun (95–120 meters in length), may also be classified as large-scale tumuli: * {{ill|Minamimori Kofun|ja|南森古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamimori Kofun|qq=}} (Nanyō, Yamagata) - Estimated to be 150–168 meters in length. and is the largest kofun from the late [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] in the Tōhoku region, dated to the mid-6th century. == Overview == Tsukamae Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus located in the eastern part of Fukushima Prefecture, in the Hamadōri area, at the head of Onahama Bay on the alluvial plain of the right bank of the Yada River (a tributary of the Fujiwara River). The mound has been significantly leveled and eroded over time.{{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} Excavations conducted by the Iwaki City Board of Education in fiscal 2016 (Heisei 28) and a survey by Fukushima University revealed its scale and construction method.{{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} The tumulus, now irregularly shaped, is reconstructed as originally being keyhole-shaped, with the front portion oriented southwest. Its mound measures 95–120 meters in length, Ranking 3rd to 8th in size among Tōhoku kofun and is the largest from the late Kofun period in the region. Remains of haniwa, including cylindrical haniwa (some morning glory-shaped) and figurative haniwa (lid and shield-shaped), have been found on the mound surface. A surrounding moat (likely horseshoe-shaped) has also been identified. While the burial facilities are unknown, past records suggest the presence of ornaments such as gyōba (杏葉). Based on the haniwa and other artifacts, the mound's construction is estimated to date to the mid-6th century, making it significant for studying the late Kofun period in eastern Japan. == Site History == * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': Excavation due to residential development. Confirmed as a large-scale kofun (Iwaki City Board of Education). * '''2017 (Heisei 29)''': Survey conducted. Confirmed as the largest late Kofun in Tōhoku (Fukushima University Archaeological Research Laboratory).["Iwaki's Tsukamae Kofun: Largest Late Kofun in Tōhoku Revealed by Survey" (Mainichi Shimbun, May 11, 2017)]
["Tsukamae Kofun: Measuring 120m, Largest Late Tōhoku Kofun?" (Kahoku Shimpō, May 11, 2017)]. == Kofun Details == Measurements from the 2017 survey are as follows: * Mound length: 95–120 meters * Rear round portion: Diameter 53 meters * Front rectangular portion: ** Length: 54–70 meters ** Width: 45–65 meters The mound was constructed by alternating layers of earth to form embankments, then filling the inner area, a technique characteristic of western Japan. Additionally, soil bags and clod stacking were used, a method typically reserved for high-status tombs in the late Kofun period. == Notes == {{Notelist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} * {{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayama Kofun|qq=}} (Yotsukura, Iwaki) - Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. 112-meter keyhole-shaped tumulus. * {{ill|Ishikami no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Ishikami no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Ishikami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|qq=}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|塚前古墳|version=99995314|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:Fukushima Prefecture history]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]]}} [[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[ja:塚前古墳]] [[sn:sukamae Kofun]] [[en:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[Category:いわき市の歴史]] [[Category:Kofun of Fukushima prefecture]]

Based on the haniwa and other artifacts, the mound's construction is estimated to date to the mid-6th century, making it significant for studying the late Kofun period in eastern Japan. == Site History == * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': Excavation due to residential development. Confirmed as a large-scale kofun (Iwaki City Board of Education). * '''2017 (Heisei 29)''': Survey conducted. Confirmed as the largest late Kofun in Tōhoku (Fukushima University Archaeological Research Laboratory).["Iwaki's Tsukamae Kofun: Largest Late Kofun in Tōhoku Revealed by Survey" (Mainichi Shimbun, May 11, 2017)]
["Tsukamae Kofun: Measuring 120m, Largest Late Tōhoku Kofun?" (Kahoku Shimpō, May 11, 2017)]. == Kofun Details == Measurements from the 2017 survey are as follows: * Mound length: 95–120 meters * Rear round portion: Diameter 53 meters * Front rectangular portion: ** Length: 54–70 meters ** Width: 45–65 meters The mound was constructed by alternating layers of earth to form embankments, then filling the inner area, a technique characteristic of western Japan. Additionally, soil bags and clod stacking were used, a method typically reserved for high-status tombs in the late Kofun period. == Notes == {{Notelist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} * {{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayama Kofun|qq=}} (Yotsukura, Iwaki) - Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. 112-meter keyhole-shaped tumulus. * {{ill|Ishikami no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Ishikami no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Ishikami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|qq=}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|塚前古墳|version=99995314|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:Fukushima Prefecture history]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]]}} [[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[ja:塚前古墳]] [[sn:sukamae Kofun]] [[Category:History of Iwaki]] [[Category:Kofun of Fukushima prefecture]]

revision.text:
'''Tsukamae Kofun''' (塚前古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Onahama Rinjo|ja|小名浜林城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onahama Rinjo|qq=}}, [[:en:Iwaki, Fukushima|Iwaki, Fukushima]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]]. Its shape is that of a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]. It is not designated as a historic site. Among kofun in the [[:en:Tōhoku region|Tōhoku region]], it Ranks 3rd to 8th in sizeMajor kofun in the Tōhoku region include: # [[:en:Raijinyama Kofun|Raijin-yama Kofun]] (Natori, Miyagi) - 168 meters in length. # [[:en:Kamegamori-Chinjumori Kofun|Kamegamori-Chinjumori Kofun]] (Aizubange, Fukushima) - 129 meters in length. # [[:en:Ōtsukayama Kofun|Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun]] (Aizuwakamatsu, Fukushima) - 114 meters in length. # {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayama Kofun|qq=}} (Iwaki, Fukushima) - 112 meters in length. # [[:en:Tōmizuka Kofun|Tōmizuka Kofun]] (Sendai, Miyagi) - 110 meters in length. # {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aotsuka Kofun|qq=}} (Ōsaki, Miyagi) - 100 meters in length. # [[:en:Inarimori Kofun|Inarimori Kofun]] (Nanyō, Yamagata) - 96 meters in length. Recent surveys suggest that other kofun, including Tsukamae Kofun (95–120 meters in length), may also be classified as large-scale tumuli: * {{ill|Minamimori Kofun|ja|南森古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamimori Kofun|qq=}} (Nanyō, Yamagata) - Estimated to be 150–168 meters in length. and is the largest kofun from the late [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] in the Tōhoku region, dated to the mid-6th century. == Overview == Tsukamae Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus located in the eastern part of Fukushima Prefecture, in the Hamadōri area, at the head of Onahama Bay on the alluvial plain of the right bank of the Yada River (a tributary of the Fujiwara River). The mound has been significantly leveled and eroded over time.{{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} Excavations conducted by the Iwaki City Board of Education in fiscal 2016 (Heisei 28) and a survey by Fukushima University revealed its scale and construction method.{{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} The tumulus, now irregularly shaped, is reconstructed as originally being keyhole-shaped, with the front portion oriented southwest. Its mound measures 95–120 meters in length, Ranking 3rd to 8th in size among Tōhoku kofun and is the largest from the late Kofun period in the region. Remains of haniwa, including cylindrical haniwa (some morning glory-shaped) and figurative haniwa (lid and shield-shaped), have been found on the mound surface. A surrounding moat (likely horseshoe-shaped) has also been identified. While the burial facilities are unknown, past records suggest the presence of ornaments such as gyōba (杏葉). Based on the haniwa and other artifacts, the mound's construction is estimated to date to the mid-6th century, making it significant for studying the late Kofun period in eastern Japan. == Site History == * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': Excavation due to residential development. Confirmed as a large-scale kofun (Iwaki City Board of Education). * '''2017 (Heisei 29)''': Survey conducted. Confirmed as the largest late Kofun in Tōhoku (Fukushima University Archaeological Research Laboratory).["Iwaki's Tsukamae Kofun: Largest Late Kofun in Tōhoku Revealed by Survey" (Mainichi Shimbun, May 11, 2017)]
["Tsukamae Kofun: Measuring 120m, Largest Late Tōhoku Kofun?" (Kahoku Shimpō, May 11, 2017)]. == Kofun Details == Measurements from the 2017 survey are as follows: * Mound length: 95–120 meters * Rear round portion: Diameter 53 meters * Front rectangular portion: ** Length: 54–70 meters ** Width: 45–65 meters The mound was constructed by alternating layers of earth to form embankments, then filling the inner area, a technique characteristic of western Japan. Additionally, soil bags and clod stacking were used, a method typically reserved for high-status tombs in the late Kofun period. == Notes == {{Notelist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} * {{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayama Kofun|qq=}} (Yotsukura, Iwaki) - Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. 112-meter keyhole-shaped tumulus. * {{ill|Ishikami no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Ishikami no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Ishikami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|qq=}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|塚前古墳|version=99995314|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]]}} [[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[ja:塚前古墳]] [[sn:sukamae Kofun]] [[Category:History of Iwaki]] [[Category:Kofun of Fukushima prefecture]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q48753149]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tsukamae Kofun''' (塚前古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Onahama Rinjo|ja|小名浜林城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onahama Rinjo|qq=}}, [[:en:Iwaki, Fukushima|Iwaki, Fukushima]], [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]. Its shape is that of a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]. It is not designated as a historic site. Among kofun in the [[:en:Tōhoku region|Tōhoku region]], it Ranks 3rd to 8th in sizeMajor kofun in the Tōhoku region include: # [[:en:Raijinyama Kofun|Raijin-yama Kofun]] (Natori, Miyagi) - 168 meters in length. # [[:en:Kamegamori-Chinjumori Kofun|Kamegamori-Chinjumori Kofun]] (Aizubange, Fukushima) - 129 meters in length. # [[:en:Ōtsukayama Kofun|Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun]] (Aizuwakamatsu, Fukushima) - 114 meters in length. # {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayama Kofun|qq=}} (Iwaki, Fukushima) - 112 meters in length. # [[:en:Tōmizuka Kofun|Tōmizuka Kofun]] (Sendai, Miyagi) - 110 meters in length. # {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aotsuka Kofun|qq=}} (Ōsaki, Miyagi) - 100 meters in length. # [[:en:Inarimori Kofun|Inarimori Kofun]] (Nanyō, Yamagata) - 96 meters in length. Recent surveys suggest that other kofun, including Tsukamae Kofun (95–120 meters in length), may also be classified as large-scale tumuli: * {{ill|Minamimori Kofun|ja|南森古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamimori Kofun|qq=}} (Nanyō, Yamagata) - Estimated to be 150–168 meters in length. and is the largest kofun from the late [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] in the Tōhoku region, dated to the mid-6th century. == Overview == Tsukamae Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus located in the eastern part of Fukushima Prefecture, in the Hamadōri area, at the head of Onahama Bay on the alluvial plain of the right bank of the Yada River (a tributary of the Fujiwara River). The mound has been significantly leveled and eroded over time.{{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} Excavations conducted by the Iwaki City Board of Education in fiscal 2016 (Heisei 28) and a survey by Fukushima University revealed its scale and construction method.{{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} The tumulus, now irregularly shaped, is reconstructed as originally being keyhole-shaped, with the front portion oriented southwest. Its mound measures 95–120 meters in length, Ranking 3rd to 8th in size among Tōhoku kofun and is the largest from the late Kofun period in the region. Remains of haniwa, including cylindrical haniwa (some morning glory-shaped) and figurative haniwa (lid and shield-shaped), have been found on the mound surface. A surrounding moat (likely horseshoe-shaped) has also been identified. While the burial facilities are unknown, past records suggest the presence of ornaments such as gyōba (杏葉). Based on the haniwa and other artifacts, the mound's construction is estimated to date to the mid-6th century, making it significant for studying the late Kofun period in eastern Japan. == Site History == * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': Excavation due to residential development. Confirmed as a large-scale kofun (Iwaki City Board of Education). * '''2017 (Heisei 29)''': Survey conducted. Confirmed as the largest late Kofun in Tōhoku (Fukushima University Archaeological Research Laboratory).["Iwaki's Tsukamae Kofun: Largest Late Kofun in Tōhoku Revealed by Survey" (Mainichi Shimbun, May 11, 2017)]
["Tsukamae Kofun: Measuring 120m, Largest Late Tōhoku Kofun?" (Kahoku Shimpō, May 11, 2017)]. == Kofun Details == Measurements from the 2017 survey are as follows: * Mound length: 95–120 meters * Rear round portion: Diameter 53 meters * Front rectangular portion: ** Length: 54–70 meters ** Width: 45–65 meters The mound was constructed by alternating layers of earth to form embankments, then filling the inner area, a technique characteristic of western Japan. Additionally, soil bags and clod stacking were used, a method typically reserved for high-status tombs in the late Kofun period. == Notes == {{Notelist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Harvnb|塚前古墳 調査概報|2017}} * {{Harvnb|福島大学 測量調査|2017}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayama Kofun|qq=}} (Yotsukura, Iwaki) - Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. 112-meter keyhole-shaped tumulus. * {{ill|Ishikami no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Ishikami no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Ishikami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|qq=}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|塚前古墳|version=99995314|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]]}} [[sn:Tsukamae Kofun]] [[ja:塚前古墳]] [[sn:sukamae Kofun]] [[Category:History of Iwaki]] [[Category:Kofun of Fukushima prefecture]] [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]]
